prompt: Completa: '____ più piano, ti sento male!' (parlare, tu)
explanation: 'Parla più piano, ti sento male!' means 'Speak more slowly, I can't hear you properly!' When you tell a friend to do something, you use the command form, the imperative. For an -are verb, the informal tu imperative ends in -a: parlare gives parla. Watch the vowel: this is NOT the present-tense tu (parli), the imperative swaps the i for an a. (-ere and -ire verbs go the other way, taking -i: prendi, dormi.)

prompt: Completa: '____ che bel tramonto!' (guardare, tu)
explanation: 'Guarda che bel tramonto!' means 'Look what a beautiful sunset!' To tell a friend to look, Italian uses the imperative, the command form. An -are verb takes -a in the informal tu imperative, so guardare gives guarda. The present-tense tu would be guardi; the imperative deliberately ends in -a instead.

prompt: Completa: '____ un attimo, arrivo subito!' (aspettare, tu)
explanation: 'Aspetta un attimo, arrivo subito!' means 'Wait a moment, I'm coming right away!' Telling someone to wait is a command, so Italian reaches for the imperative. The informal tu imperative of an -are verb ends in -a: aspettare gives aspetta. Don't borrow the present tu aspetti here, the imperative form is aspetta.

prompt: Completa: '____ tutte le verdure!' (mangiare, tu)
explanation: 'Mangia tutte le verdure!' means 'Eat all your vegetables!' A parent telling a child to eat uses the imperative, the command form. The informal tu imperative of an -are verb ends in -a, so mangiare gives mangia. The stem already ends in i (mangi-), and the -a simply follows it: mangia, written with a single i. The present-tense tu would be mangi.

prompt: Completa: '____ l'ombrello, sta per piovere!' (prendere, tu)
explanation: 'Prendi l'ombrello, sta per piovere!' means 'Take the umbrella, it's about to rain!' Telling a friend to grab something is a command, hence the imperative. An -ere verb forms the informal tu imperative in -i: prendere gives prendi. This is the mirror image of the -are rule (parla): -ere and -ire end in -i, -are ends in -a. Writing prenda here would wrongly borrow the -are vowel.

prompt: Completa: '____ il tuo nome qui!' (scrivere, tu)
explanation: 'Scrivi il tuo nome qui!' means 'Write your name here!' To tell a friend to write, you use the imperative. An -ere verb takes -i in the informal tu imperative, so scrivere gives scrivi. Keep the vowel as -i (not the -a of the -are verbs): scriva would be the wrong, -are-style ending.

prompt: Completa: '____ bene, ci vediamo domani!' (dormire, tu)
explanation: 'Dormi bene, ci vediamo domani!' means 'Sleep well, see you tomorrow!' Wishing someone a good sleep with a command uses the imperative. An -ire verb forms the informal tu imperative in -i, just like the -ere verbs: dormire gives dormi. The -a ending (dorma) belongs to the -are verbs, not here.

prompt: Completa: '____ la finestra, fa caldo!' (aprire, tu)
explanation: 'Apri la finestra, fa caldo!' means 'Open the window, it's hot!' Telling a friend to open the window is a command, so the imperative is used. An -ire verb takes -i in the informal tu imperative: aprire gives apri. As with all -ere/-ire verbs the vowel is -i, never the -a of the -are group.

prompt: Completa: '____ i compiti prima di uscire!' (finire, tu)
explanation: 'Finisci i compiti prima di uscire!' means 'Finish your homework before you go out!' A command to finish uses the imperative. Finire is an -isc- verb: it inserts -isc- between the stem and the ending in the tu form, so the informal tu imperative is finisci (fin- + -isc- + -i). The plain -i ending still applies (it is an -ire verb), the -isc- just sits in front of it.

prompt: Completa: '____ di qualcos'altro, questo argomento è noioso!' (parlare, noi)
explanation: 'Parliamo di qualcos'altro, questo argomento è noioso!' means 'Let's talk about something else, this subject is boring!' A 'let's...' invitation uses the imperative in the noi form. Here the imperative simply equals the present-tense noi: parlare gives parliamo, meaning 'let's talk'. There is no special ending to learn, the noi imperative borrows the ordinary present form.

prompt: Completa: '____ a casa, è già tardi!' (andare, noi)
explanation: 'Andiamo a casa, è già tardi!' means 'Let's go home, it's already late!' The 'let's go' invitation uses the noi imperative, which equals the present-tense noi. Andare gives andiamo, 'let's go'. Even though andare is irregular elsewhere, its noi imperative is just the everyday present noi form.

prompt: Completa: '____ qui, torno tra un minuto!' (aspettare, voi)
explanation: 'Aspettate qui, torno tra un minuto!' means 'Wait here, I'll be back in a minute!' Telling a group to wait uses the voi imperative, which equals the present-tense voi: aspettare gives aspettate. Like the noi imperative, the voi form needs no special ending, it reuses the ordinary present.

prompt: Completa: '____ la risposta sul foglio!' (scrivere, voi)
explanation: 'Scrivete la risposta sul foglio!' means 'Write the answer on the sheet of paper!' A command to a group to write uses the voi imperative, equal to the present-tense voi: scrivere gives scrivete. The -ete ending is the ordinary present voi of an -ere verb; the voi imperative simply reuses it.

prompt: Completa la forma negativa: '____ così in fretta, ascolta!' (parlare, tu, negativo)
explanation: 'Non parlare così in fretta, ascolta!' means 'Don't talk so fast, listen!' To tell a friend NOT to do something, Italian does something surprising: the negative tu command is non plus the infinitive. So 'don't speak' is non parlare, using the dictionary form parlare, not the positive command parla. A learner who negates the positive form (non parla) has made the classic error; the negative tu always reaches for the infinitive.
examiner_note: Marker target is the whole phrase 'non parlare'. must_not catches non+positive (non parla) and non+present (non parli).

prompt: Completa la forma negativa: '____ quella strada, è chiusa!' (prendere, tu, negativo)
explanation: 'Non prendere quella strada, è chiusa!' means 'Don't take that road, it's closed!' A 'don't...' command to a friend uses non plus the infinitive. For prendere that is non prendere. The same rule covers every conjugation: the negative tu command is built from the infinitive, never from the positive imperative (so not non prendi).

prompt: Completa la forma negativa: '____ troppo tardi, domani lavori!' (dormire, tu, negativo)
explanation: 'Non dormire troppo tardi, domani lavori!' means 'Don't sleep too late, you're working tomorrow!' Telling a friend not to sleep late is a negative command, so Italian uses non plus the infinitive: non dormire. The infinitive dormire stands in for any positive form; non dormi (negating the present) is the typical slip the rule guards against.

prompt: Completa la forma negativa: '____ la porta a nessuno!' (aprire, tu, negativo)
explanation: 'Non aprire la porta a nessuno!' means 'Don't open the door to anyone!' A negative command to a friend is non plus the infinitive. For aprire that gives non aprire, 'don't open'. The infinitive is the engine of the negative tu form, so non apri (the negated present) is wrong.

prompt: Completa la forma negativa: '____ rumore, il bambino dorme!' (fare, tu, negativo)

markpoints.label: negative tu = non + infinitive even for irregulars (non fare)

explanation: 'Non fare rumore, il bambino dorme!' means 'Don't make any noise, the baby is sleeping!' Here is the payoff of the non + infinitive rule: even a verb with an irregular positive command obeys it. Fare has the short positive tu fa', but its negative tu command is the plain infinitive: non fare. So the irregular short forms (fa', va', di') vanish in the negative, replaced by the infinitive. Non fa' and non fai are both wrong.
examiner_note: Bridges to irregular_tu: the apocopated fa' is replaced by the infinitive under negation. must_not catches non fa / non fai / non fa'.

prompt: Completa la forma negativa: '____ di politica a tavola!' (parlare, noi, negativo)
explanation: 'Non parliamo di politica a tavola!' means 'Let's not talk about politics at the table!' The noi negative command is simpler than the tu one: it is just non plus the ordinary affirmative. Parlare gives the positive noi parliamo, so the negative is non parliamo, 'let's not talk'. The non + infinitive trick is unique to the tu form; noi and voi negate their normal command. Watch out: non parlare would wrongly use the tu negative.

prompt: Completa la forma negativa: '____ ancora, dobbiamo andare!' (aspettare, voi, negativo)
explanation: 'Non aspettate ancora, dobbiamo andare!' means 'Don't wait any longer, we have to go!' The voi negative command, like the noi one, is simply non plus the affirmative: aspettare gives the positive voi aspettate, so 'don't wait' is non aspettate. Only the tu form uses non + infinitive; for voi you negate the normal command, so non aspettare would be wrong here.

prompt: Completa: '____ a letto, è ora di dormire!' (andare, tu)
explanation: 'Va' a letto, è ora di dormire!' means 'Go to bed, it's time to sleep!' A handful of very common verbs have a special short tu command. Andare is one: its informal tu imperative is the clipped form va' (with an apostrophe), and the full vai is also accepted. Both mean 'go!'. The apostrophe marks the truncation; the longer vai is the everyday alternative.
examiner_note: Accept va' and vai at full credit (dispatch flags the apostrophe/long-form acceptance for the project author). Bare regularised 'va' without apostrophe is a residual miss the substring marker cannot cleanly separate; see coverage notes.

prompt: Completa: '____ una mano a tua sorella!' (dare, tu)
explanation: 'Da' una mano a tua sorella!' means 'Give your sister a hand!' Dare belongs to the small set of verbs with a short tu command. Its informal tu imperative is da' (apostrophed), with the full dai also accepted, both meaning 'give!'. As with va'/vai, the apostrophe shows the clipped form and the longer one is the alternative.
examiner_note: Accept da' and dai at full credit.

prompt: Completa: '____ silenzio, per favore!' (fare, tu)
explanation: 'Fa' silenzio, per favore!' means 'Be quiet, please!' Fare is another verb with a short tu command: the informal tu imperative is fa' (apostrophed) or the full fai, both meaning 'do!' or 'make!'. The clipped fa' carries an apostrophe to mark the missing ending; fai is the longer accepted form. (Note faccia is the formal Lei form, not this.)
examiner_note: Accept fa' and fai at full credit.

prompt: Completa: '____ fermo un momento!' (stare, tu)
explanation: 'Sta' fermo un momento!' means 'Keep still for a moment!' Stare has a short tu command like its cousins: the informal tu imperative is sta' (apostrophed) or the full stai, both telling someone to 'stay' or 'keep' a state ('stay still'). The apostrophe again marks the clipped form, with stai as the longer option.
examiner_note: Accept sta' and stai at full credit. Bare 'sta' matches inside stai/stava; any_phrase keeps the apostrophe form. See coverage on apostrophe normalisation.

prompt: Completa: '____ sempre la verità!' (dire, tu)

markpoints.label: irregular tu: dire -> di' (no long form)

any_phrases.note: Dated spelling of the apocopated imperative. The form is right; modern standard writes di' with an apostrophe.

explanation: 'Di' sempre la verità!' means 'Always tell the truth!' Dire has the shortest command of all: the informal tu imperative is di', 'say!' or 'tell!'. Unlike va'/vai or fa'/fai, dire has NO long alternative, di' is the only form, always written with the apostrophe. The present-tense tu dici is a different word and not a command. (You may see di' written dì in older texts; the modern standard is di', with an apostrophe marking the clipped form.)
examiner_note: di' has no long form. The dated spelling dì takes graded credit 0.7 per Rev 20(i): the apocopated form is correctly reached, the miss is orthographic only, and accent_load_bearing keeps the fold from rescuing it to full credit. Thread: Architecture_ImperativoAuthor_misconception_tagging_schema v4.

prompt: Completa: '____ gentile con i nonni!' (essere, tu)
explanation: 'Sii gentile con i nonni!' means 'Be kind to your grandparents!' Essere has its own irregular tu command: sii, 'be!'. It is not clipped like va' or fa', it is simply an irregular form to memorise. Don't confuse it with the present-tense sei ('you are') or with the formal sia; the informal command is sii.
examiner_note: any_phrase 'sii' is not a substring of sei/sia, so must_not catches both cleanly.

prompt: Completa: '____ pazienza, arriverà presto!' (avere, tu)
explanation: 'Abbi pazienza, arriverà presto!' means 'Be patient, he'll be here soon!' Avere also has an irregular tu command: abbi, as in abbi pazienza ('be patient', literally 'have patience'). Like sii, it is a form to learn rather than a clipped one. The present-tense hai ('you have') and the formal abbia are different; the informal command is abbi.
examiner_note: CAUTION: any_phrase 'abbi' is a substring of the formal form 'abbia'. match_at:'end' is set so that, where the engine supports end-anchoring, the Lei form abbia is not mis-credited on this tu item. If end-anchoring is NOT yet supported, the positive-first marker would match 'abbi' inside 'abbia' and wrongly credit the register-confusion; must_not_include cannot override a positive match. Flagged to the project author in coverage (apostrophe/short-form marker question).

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: 'Signora, ____ più lentamente, per favore!' (parlare, Lei)
explanation: 'Signora, parli più lentamente, per favore!' means 'Madam, please speak more slowly!' When you give a polite command to someone you address as Lei, Italian uses the present subjunctive form. For parlare that subjunctive is parli, so the formal command is parli. It looks like the present-tense tu (parli) but it is doing different work: the informal command would be parla, while the respectful Lei command is parli.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo: the form IS the present subjunctive of parlare.

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: 'Signor Verdi, ____ questa medicina due volte al giorno!' (prendere, Lei)
explanation: 'Signor Verdi, prenda questa medicina due volte al giorno!' means 'Mr Verdi, take this medicine twice a day!' A polite command to a person you address as Lei uses the present subjunctive. Prendere's subjunctive is prenda, so the formal command is prenda. Note the vowel flips relative to the informal: the tu command is prendi (in -i), while the Lei command is prenda (in -a), exactly the reverse of the regular indicative pattern.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo.

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: 'Signor Bianchi, ____ pure, l'ascolto!' (dire, Lei)
explanation: 'Signor Bianchi, dica pure, l'ascolto!' means 'Mr Bianchi, do go ahead, I'm listening!' The polite Lei command uses the present subjunctive even for irregular verbs. Dire's subjunctive is dica, so the formal 'go ahead and speak' is dica. This is built from the subjunctive stem (dic-), not from the infinitive, and it contrasts with the informal di'.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo (irregular subjunctive dica).

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: 'Prego, ____ avanti, si accomodi!' (venire, Lei)
explanation: 'Prego, venga avanti, si accomodi!' means 'Please come forward and take a seat!' For a polite Lei command Italian borrows the present subjunctive, and irregular verbs follow their irregular subjunctive. Venire's subjunctive is venga, so 'come in' addressed respectfully is venga. It is built from the subjunctive stem (veng-), not from the informal vieni.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo (irregular subjunctive venga).

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: 'Per favore, ____ con calma, non c'è fretta!' (fare, Lei)
explanation: 'Per favore, faccia con calma, non c'è fretta!' means 'Please take your time, there's no hurry!' A polite Lei command uses the present subjunctive form. Fare's subjunctive is faccia, so the respectful command is faccia. It comes from the subjunctive stem (facci-) and has nothing in common with the informal short form fa'.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo (irregular subjunctive faccia).

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: '____ sempre dritto fino al semaforo!' (andare, Lei)
explanation: 'Vada sempre dritto fino al semaforo!' means 'Go straight on as far as the traffic light!' Giving polite directions to someone you address as Lei calls for the present subjunctive. Andare's subjunctive is vada, so 'go straight on' addressed respectfully is vada. As always for the Lei command, the form is the subjunctive (vada), not the informal short va'.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo (irregular subjunctive vada).

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: '____ puntuale all'appuntamento, per favore!' (essere, Lei)
explanation: 'Sia puntuale all'appuntamento, per favore!' means 'Please be on time for the appointment!' The polite Lei command of essere is its present subjunctive, sia: 'be on time'. It is a different form from the informal command sii and from the present-tense sei. For the respectful register, reach for the subjunctive sia.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo (irregular subjunctive sia).

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: '____ pazienza, signora, la pratica è quasi pronta!' (avere, Lei)
explanation: 'Abbia pazienza, signora, la pratica è quasi pronta!' means 'Please be patient, madam, the paperwork is almost ready!' The polite Lei command of avere is its present subjunctive, abbia, as in abbia pazienza ('be patient'). It contrasts with the informal abbi: same verb, but the respectful register uses the subjunctive abbia.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo. Positive-first ordering is safe here: the correct 'abbia' matches before the must_not 'abbi' (a substring of it) is checked, and a learner writing the informal 'abbi' gets no positive match so it is caught.

prompt: Completa la forma di cortesia: 'Signor Rossi, ____ bene stanotte!' (dormire, Lei)
explanation: 'Signor Rossi, dorma bene stanotte!' means 'Mr Rossi, sleep well tonight!' A polite Lei command uses the present subjunctive. Dormire's subjunctive is dorma, so the respectful 'sleep well' is dorma. The informal command would be dormi (in -i); the Lei command flips to -a (dorma), the subjunctive ending.
examiner_note: Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo.

prompt: Completa la forma molto formale rivolta a più persone: 'Signori, ____ pure, prego!' (entrare, Loro)

translation.reveal: entrare - to enter / to come in

explanation: 'Signori, entrino pure, prego!' means 'Gentlemen, do come in, please!' The very formal Loro command (addressing several people with great deference) uses the third-person plural present subjunctive. Entrare's 3pl subjunctive is entrino, so 'do come in' in this archaic register is entrino. It is largely literary today, the everyday equivalent being the voi command entrate; this item simply shows where the form lives.
examiner_note: Loro imperative is archaic/literary; included as a single low-frequency item per dispatch. Cross-reference verb_form.congiuntivo (3pl subjunctive).

prompt: Un cliente esita sulla soglia; lo inviti a entrare con cortesia: 'Prego, ____!' (entrare, Lei, + un marcatore che dà via libera)

translation.reveal: entrare - to enter / to come in

explanation: 'Entri pure!' means 'Do come in!'. A bare command can sound abrupt, so Italian softens it. The particle pure after an imperative gives permission and warmth, turning an order into an invitation ('go right ahead'). The verb still carries the register: to someone you address as Lei the form is entri, and pure follows it.
examiner_note: Cross-credit (Rev 27): pure = the softening skill, entri = the formal form. Wrong-register 'entra' is tagged register.tu_lei_mismatch on the formation markpoint.

prompt: Un ospite chiede se può servirsi da solo; glielo concedi con gentilezza: 'Certo, ____!' (fare, Lei, + un marcatore che dà via libera)
explanation: 'Faccia pure!' means 'Go right ahead!'. Faccia is the command form for someone you address as Lei, and pure softens it into permission rather than an order. It is the standard warm way to tell a guest to help themselves.

prompt: Un amico chiede se può prendere una fetta di torta; glielo concedi: 'Certo, ____!' (prendere, tu, + un marcatore che dà via libera)
explanation: 'Prendi pure!' means 'Go ahead and take some!'. To a friend (tu) the form is prendi, and pure turns the command into a friendly go-ahead. Same softener as the formal faccia pure, just with the informal verb.

prompt: Un signore vuole dirti una cosa ma esita; lo incoraggi a parlare: 'Mi ____ ____!' (dire, Lei, + un marcatore che dà via libera)
explanation: 'Mi dica pure!' means 'Please, do tell me!'. Dica is the form for someone you address as Lei, and pure invites them to go ahead without hesitation. It is the classic gentle prompt a shopkeeper or clerk uses.
examiner_note: 'mi' is a clitic (pronoun tree); the markpoints target dica + pure only, not clitic placement.

prompt: Un amico sta per rinunciare a una gara; lo incoraggi a insistere: '____, ce la puoi fare!' (marcatore di incoraggiamento, informale)
explanation: 'Dai, ce la puoi fare!' means 'Come on, you can do it!'. Some imperatives have frozen into pure encouragement markers: dai (literally 'give'), su ('up'), forza ('strength') and coraggio all mean 'come on' here, with no trace of their literal sense. Any of them fits; the skill is reaching for the marker, not conjugating a verb.
examiner_note: Interchangeable encouragement set accepted at full credit; the leaf tests deploying a frozen marker, not a specific form. No misconception tag (a lexical choice among synonyms is not a miss).

prompt: I bambini indugiano e dovete uscire; li solleciti con affetto ma con un po' di fretta: '____, andiamo, è tardi!' (marcatore di sollecito, informale)
explanation: 'Su, andiamo!' means 'Come on, let's go!'. Su, dai and forza all work as a nudge to get moving. Like the encouragement use, these are frozen markers: su here has nothing to do with 'up', it simply chivvies.

prompt: Vuoi introdurre un'obiezione mentre parli con un amico: '____, non sono d'accordo.' (marcatore per prendere la parola, informale)
explanation: 'Senti, non sono d'accordo' means 'Look, I don't agree'. Senti ('listen') and guarda ('look') have frozen into markers for taking the floor or flagging a point, softening what follows. The plain ascolta ('listen') is the literal verb, not this discourse marker, so it does not fit here.

prompt: Fermi un signore per strada per chiedere un'informazione: '____, scusi, sa dov'è la stazione?' (marcatore per attirare l'attenzione, formale)
explanation: 'Senta, scusi...' means 'Excuse me...' (literally 'listen'). To get the attention of someone you address as Lei, the frozen marker takes the formal form senta, not the informal senti. It is the standard polite opener to a stranger. Using senti to a stranger mixes the registers.
examiner_note: Cross-credit: senta as the softening/attention marker AND as the formal form; senti here is a register slip, tagged register.tu_lei_mismatch.

prompt: Vuoi sottolineare un punto a un amico che dubita di te: '____, te lo giuro, è vero.' (marcatore per enfatizzare, informale)
explanation: 'Guarda, te lo giuro' means 'Look, I swear'. Guarda ('look') has frozen into an emphasis marker that presses a point home; senti works the same way. Neither is really telling the friend to look or listen.

prompt: Un amico ha paura di buttarsi in piscina; lo incoraggi con una parola sola: '____!' (marcatore di incoraggiamento, informale)
explanation: 'Dai!' means 'Come on!' / 'Go on!'. On its own, dai (or forza, su, coraggio) is a complete word of encouragement. These frozen markers carry the push all by themselves, with no verb to conjugate.

prompt: Su un cartello sulla porta di un negozio, per dire 'push': '____' (spingere, istruzione impersonale su un cartello)
explanation: 'Spingere' means 'Push'. On signs and in instructions addressed to no one in particular, Italian uses the bare infinitive, not a personal command. So a door says Spingere ('push') and Tirare ('pull'), never spingi or spinga. The infinitive is the impersonal, everyone-and-no-one form.
examiner_note: Breadcrumb 'impersonal infinitive' names the form-class under test -> suppress (criterion 15).

prompt: Sull'altra porta, il cartello per dire 'pull': '____' (tirare, istruzione impersonale su un cartello)
explanation: 'Tirare' means 'Pull'. Like Spingere, it is the bare infinitive: signs give impersonal instructions with the infinitive, not with a tu or Lei command.

prompt: In una ricetta, la prima istruzione: '____ bene gli ingredienti.' (mescolare, istruzione impersonale in una ricetta)
explanation: 'Mescolare bene gli ingredienti' means 'Mix the ingredients well'. Recipes address no particular reader, so each step is a bare infinitive: Mescolare ('mix'), Aggiungere ('add'), Cuocere ('cook'). It is the same impersonal instruction form used on signs.

prompt: Istruzioni sulla confezione: '____ a fuoco lento per venti minuti.' (cuocere, istruzione impersonale)
explanation: 'Cuocere a fuoco lento' means 'Cook over a low heat'. Packet and recipe instructions use the bare infinitive throughout, so it is cuocere, not the personal cuoci or cuocia.

prompt: Un cartello di divieto in un locale pubblico: '____' (fumare, divieto impersonale su un cartello)

markpoints.label: impersonal negative infinitive on a sign

explanation: 'Non fumare' means 'No smoking'. Public prohibitions use non + the bare infinitive, the impersonal form, never a personal command like non fumi. It matches the affirmative sign form (Spingere): infinitive throughout.

prompt: Passo successivo della ricetta: '____ il sale e il pepe.' (aggiungere, istruzione impersonale in una ricetta)
explanation: 'Aggiungere il sale e il pepe' means 'Add the salt and pepper'. Another recipe step, another bare infinitive: the impersonal instruction form stays consistent from line to line.

prompt: Avvertenza sull'etichetta di un prodotto: '____ in luogo fresco e asciutto.' (conservare, istruzione impersonale)
explanation: 'Conservare in luogo fresco e asciutto' means 'Store in a cool, dry place'. Product labels speak to no one in particular, so the instruction is the bare infinitive conservare, not a personal command.

prompt: Un cartello accanto a un'opera in un museo: '____' (toccare, divieto impersonale su un cartello)

markpoints.label: impersonal negative infinitive on a sign

explanation: 'Non toccare' means 'Do not touch'. As with 'Non fumare', a public prohibition uses non + the infinitive, the impersonal form that addresses every visitor and none in particular.

prompt: Signora, per favore, ____ più lentamente, non La capisco! (parlare)
explanation: 'Signora, parli più lentamente!' means 'Madam, please speak more slowly!'. Signora signals someone you address as Lei, so the imperative takes the formal form parli, not the informal parla. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.
examiner_note: Register cue = 'Signora' (formal). Cross-credit: register choice + formal formation.

prompt: Signor Rossi, ____ un momento, arrivo subito! (aspettare)
explanation: 'Signor Rossi, aspetti un momento!' means 'Mr Rossi, wait a moment!'. Addressing 'Signor Rossi' is formal (Lei), so the command is aspetti, not the informal aspetta. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Mi scusi, ____ sempre dritto fino al semaforo. (andare)
explanation: 'Mi scusi, vada sempre dritto.' means 'Excuse me, go straight on.'. 'Mi scusi' to a stranger is formal, so andare takes the Lei form vada, not the informal va'/vai. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Professore, ____ con calma, non c'è fretta. (fare)
explanation: 'Professore, faccia con calma.' means 'Professor, take your time.'. Addressing a 'Professore' is formal, so fare takes faccia, the Lei form, not fa'/fai. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Dottore, ____ pazienza, la pratica è quasi pronta. (avere)
explanation: 'Dottore, abbia pazienza.' means 'Doctor, please be patient.'. 'Dottore' is a formal address, so avere takes abbia, not the informal abbi. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Signor Bianchi, ____ gentile, mi passi il documento. (essere)
explanation: 'Signor Bianchi, sia gentile.' means 'Mr Bianchi, please be kind.'. 'Signor Bianchi' is formal (Lei), so essere takes sia, not the informal sii. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Signora, ____ pure una sedia, si accomodi. (prendere)
explanation: 'Signora, prenda pure una sedia.' means 'Madam, do take a seat.'. Addressing 'Signora' is formal, so prendere takes prenda, not the informal prendi. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Marco, ____ più piano, ti sento benissimo! (parlare)
explanation: 'Marco, parla più piano!' means 'Marco, speak more quietly!'. Marco is addressed by first name, so this is informal (tu): the command is parla, not the formal parli. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Anna, ____ la finestra, per favore, fa caldo! (aprire)
explanation: 'Anna, apri la finestra!' means 'Anna, open the window!'. A first name signals the informal tu, so aprire takes apri, not the formal apra. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Luca, ____ a casa, è tardi ormai. (andare)
explanation: 'Luca, va' a casa.' means 'Luca, go home.'. Luca is a friend (tu), so andare takes the informal va' (or vai), not the formal vada. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Gianni, ____ gentile con tua sorella! (essere)
explanation: 'Gianni, sii gentile!' means 'Gianni, be kind!'. A first name is informal (tu), so essere takes sii, not the formal sia. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Tesoro, ____ pazienza, arriviamo tra poco. (avere)
explanation: 'Tesoro, abbi pazienza.' means 'Sweetheart, be patient.'. 'Tesoro' to a loved one is informal (tu), so avere takes abbi, not the formal abbia. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Sofia, ____ ferma un attimo, ti allaccio la scarpa. (stare)
explanation: 'Sofia, sta' ferma un attimo.' means 'Sofia, keep still for a moment.'. Sofia is addressed informally (tu), so stare takes sta' (or stai), not the formal stia. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.

prompt: Dai Paolo, ____ il tuo numero qui, così ti chiamo. (scrivere)
explanation: 'Paolo, scrivi il tuo numero qui.' means 'Paolo, write your number here.'. A first name signals the informal tu, so scrivere takes scrivi, not the formal scriva. You have to read the register from who is being addressed and produce the matching form.
